answer:
MCP tools work as part of the Model Context Protocol (MCP), which is an open standard that allows AI applications to connect to tools and data sources uniformly. An MCP server exposes tools (functions the model can call), resources (readable data), and prompts (reusable templates). An MCP client embedded in the AI application connects to one or more servers, discovers their offerings, and exposes those tools to the AI agent.

The key mechanism is a handshake where the client asks the server to list its tools, each described with a name, human-readable description, and a JSON schema for arguments. The AI agent uses this information to decide which tool to call and how to fill in arguments. When the agent calls a tool, the client forwards the request to the server, which runs the tool and returns the result back to the model.

This standardized interface allows the same MCP server to be reused across different agents and frameworks, enabling independent evolution of tools and agents and easy addition of new capabilities by connecting new servers.

In summary, MCP tools work by providing a standardized, discoverable, and callable interface for AI agents to interact with external functions and data sources in a controlled loop until the agent has enough information to respond.

data.plan: Decide which tools to call — search the knowledge base, run a calculation, check the time — then answer.

tools.name: search_knowledge_base
tools.description: Search the curated AI-engineering documentation corpus (vector RAG). It covers ONLY these topics: large language models and tokens, embeddings and vector search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), AI agents and the ReAct loop, the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and prompt engineering — plus any documents the user has uploaded. Use it to ground answers about those subjects. Do NOT use it for real-world facts, current events, news, sports, people, prices, or anything outside AI engineering — for those, use `web_search`. This corpus always returns its closest passages even when nothing is truly relevant, so only call it on-topic.

tools.name: calculator
tools.description: Evaluate a basic arithmetic expression, e.g. '2 + 2' or '12 * (3 + 1)'.

tools.name: current_time
tools.description: Return the current date and time in UTC.

tools.name: kb_lookup
tools.description: Look up a one-line glossary definition for an AI engineering topic.

tools.name: load_skill
tools.description:
Load the full instructions (body) of a named skill from the catalog.

    Call this with the exact `name` of a skill from the advertised skills list when
    it fits the user's request, then follow the loaded instructions.

tools.name: web_search
tools.description:
Search the live internet and return a synthesized answer plus top sources.

    This is the right tool for ANY question outside AI engineering — real-world
    facts, current events, news, sports, people, prices, specific documentation.
    Prefer it over `search_knowledge_base` whenever the question is not about LLMs,
    embeddings, RAG, agents, MCP, or prompting. Pass a focused natural-language
    `query`.

tools.name: search_knowledge_base
tools.description: Search the curated AI-engineering documentation corpus (vector RAG). It covers ONLY these topics: large language models and tokens, embeddings and vector search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), AI agents and the ReAct loop, the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and prompt engineering — plus any documents the user has uploaded. Use it to ground answers about those subjects. Do NOT use it for real-world facts, current events, news, sports, people, prices, or anything outside AI engineering — for those, use `web_search`. This corpus always returns its closest passages even when nothing is truly relevant, so only call it on-topic.

query.description: The search query to embed and match against the knowledge base.

tools.description: Evaluate a basic arithmetic expression, e.g. '2 + 2' or '12 * (3 + 1)'.

tools.description: Return the current date and time in UTC.

tools.description: Look up a one-line glossary definition for an AI engineering topic.

    it fits the user's request, then follow the loaded instructions.

tools.description:
Search the live internet and return a synthesized answer plus top sources.

    This is the right tool for ANY question outside AI engineering — real-world
    facts, current events, news, sports, people, prices, specific documentation.
    Prefer it over `search_knowledge_base` whenever the question is not about LLMs,
    embeddings, RAG, agents, MCP, or prompting. Pass a focused natural-language
    `query`.

data.system:
You are Agent Simulator. AI Agent Simulator — explore how an agent works.

Platform guardrails (apply to every agent):
- Be helpful, accurate and concise.
- When you used retrieved context or a tool result, rely on it and say so; do not invent facts.
- If you do not have enough information, say so plainly rather than guess.
- Refuse to help with illegal, harmful, deceptive or unsafe requests.
- Prefer plain prose; use bullet lists only when the user asks for them or the content is genuinely a list.


You are the assistant inside an "AI Agent Simulator", a teaching tool that visualizes how an agentic application works.

You have tools available and you decide, on your own, whether and when to call them:
- `search_knowledge_base` is your PRIMARY knowledge tool. For any question about a concept, how something works, a comparison, or anything that could be documented, you MUST call `search_knowledge_base` first and ground your answer in what it returns — do not answer such questions from memory alone.
- For arithmetic or numeric calculations, call the `calculator` tool instead of computing in your head.
- For the current date or time, call `current_time`.
- `kb_lookup` only returns a single canned one-line glossary string for a few basic terms; prefer `search_knowledge_base` for anything more than a trivial one-word definition.


You also have access to these reusable Skills. When one fits the user's request, call the `load_skill` tool with its exact name to load its full instructions, then follow them:
- explicar-para-iniciante: Use quando o usuário pedir uma explicação simples, 'para iniciante', 'sem jargão' ou 'como se eu tivesse 5 anos'.
- glossario-ao-final: Use quando o usuário pedir um glossário ou quando a resposta usar termos técnicos.
- resumo-em-bullets: Use quando o usuário pedir um resumo, 'em tópicos' ou bullet points.

chunks.text:
# Model Context Protocol (MCP)

The Model Context Protocol is an open standard that lets AI applications connect
to tools and data sources in a uniform way. Before MCP, every integration was
bespoke: each app wired up each API by hand. MCP defines a common protocol so a
single client can talk to many interchangeable servers.

An **MCP server** exposes capabilities — most importantly *tools* (functions the
model can call), but also *resources* (readable data) and *prompts* (reusable
templates). An **MCP client**, embedded in the AI application, connects to one
or more servers, discovers what they offer, and exposes those tools to the
agent. Communication happens over a transport such as stdio (for local servers
launched as subprocesses) or HTTP/SSE (for remote servers).
chunks.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP)

chunks.text:
to the
agent. Communication happens over a transport such as stdio (for local servers
launched as subprocesses) or HTTP/SSE (for remote servers).

The handshake is what makes MCP powerful. When a client connects, it asks the
server to list its tools. Each tool comes with a name, a human-readable
description, and a JSON schema describing its arguments. The agent uses these
descriptions to decide which tool to call and how to fill in the arguments. When
the agent calls a tool, the client forwards the request to the server, the
server runs it, and the result flows back to the model.

Because the interface is standardized, the same MCP server can be reused across
different agents and frameworks. This decoupling is the main benefit: tools and
agents evolve independently, and adding a capability is as simple as connecting
another server.
chunks.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP)

chunks.text:
until the model
decides to answer or a step limit is reached. The step limit is important: it
prevents runaway loops and bounds cost and latency.

Frameworks like LangGraph model this loop as a graph of nodes. A typical graph
has a node that calls the model, a conditional edge that checks whether the
model requested a tool, a tool-execution node, and an edge that loops back to
the model node. Modeling the agent as an explicit state machine makes it easy to
add memory, branching, retries, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints.

Good agent design is mostly about constraints: a clear system prompt, a small
set of well-described tools, and sensible limits. Too many tools confuse the
model; vague tool descriptions lead to wrong calls. The art is giving the model
just enough freedom to be useful without letting it wander.
chunks.title: AI Agents and the ReAct Loop

chunks.text:
# AI Agents and the ReAct Loop

An AI agent is a language model wrapped in a control loop that lets it take
actions, observe the results, and decide what to do next. Rather than producing
a single answer in one shot, an agent can call tools, read their output, and
iterate until it has enough information to respond.

The most common pattern is **ReAct** (Reason + Act). On each turn the model
either emits a tool call (an action) or a final answer. When it emits a tool
call, the orchestrator runs the tool and feeds the result back into the
conversation, then asks the model again. The loop continues until the model
decides to answer or a step limit is reached. The step limit is important: it
prevents runaway loops and bounds cost and latency.
chunks.title: AI Agents and the ReAct Loop

MCP tools work as part of the Model Context Protocol (MCP), which is an open standard that allows AI applications to connect to tools and data sources uniformly. An MCP server exposes tools (functions the model can call), resources (readable data), and prompts (reusable templates). An MCP client embedded in the AI application connects to one or more servers, discovers their offerings, and exposes those tools to the AI agent.
